title: Numeric Types, Memory Layouts, and ML Data Formats
key_insight: Data type choice directly determines memory use, numerical stability, and hardware compatibility in production AI.
body:
## NumPy and PyTorch Data Types

```python
import numpy as np
import torch

# NumPy dtypes:
np.float32  # standard ML precision — 4 bytes
np.float16  # half precision — 2 bytes
np.int32    # integer — 4 bytes
np.uint8    # pixel values (0-255) — 1 byte
np.bool_    # boolean — 1 byte

# PyTorch dtypes:
torch.float32     # aka torch.float
torch.float16     # aka torch.half
torch.bfloat16    # brain float — better range than float16
torch.int64       # aka torch.long — for indices
torch.bool        # for masks
```

## When to Use Each Type

```python
# Image pixel data: uint8 (saves 4× memory vs float32)
image = np.array(PIL.Image.open('photo.jpg'), dtype=np.uint8)

# Normalise for model input: float32
image_float = image.astype(np.float32) / 255.0

# Token IDs and indices: int64 (long)
token_ids = torch.tensor([101, 2023, 1996], dtype=torch.long)

# Attention mask (0/1): bool or int8
mask = torch.tensor([True, True, False], dtype=torch.bool)

# Model weights: float32 (training) or float16 (inference)
model = model.to(torch.float16)  # half-precision inference
```

## Memory Layout: Contiguous vs Non-Contiguous

```python
# Contiguous tensor: elements are adjacent in memory (fast)
x = torch.randn(4, 4)
print(x.is_contiguous())  # True

# Non-contiguous after transpose: elements not adjacent
y = x.T  # transpose
print(y.is_contiguous())  # False

# CUDA operations often require contiguous tensors:
y_contiguous = y.contiguous()  # copies to new memory layout
print(y_contiguous.is_contiguous())  # True
```

## Structured Data Formats for AI

```
CSV        → pd.read_csv()    — tabular data
JPEG/PNG   → PIL.Image        — images (uint8)
npy        → np.load()        — numpy arrays (any dtype)
Parquet    → pd.read_parquet() — compressed columnar data
HDF5       → h5py             — large array datasets
JSONL      → json.loads()     — text data, one JSON per line
```
common_mistakes: Keeping images as float32 in a dataset — uint8 uses 4× less memory; only convert to float right before model input
Using default Python int (64-bit) for index tensors — PyTorch embedding layers require torch.long (int64)
Calling .transpose() without .contiguous() before CUDA ops — causes cryptic errors about non-contiguous tensors
mini_exercise: Load an image with PIL, convert to numpy uint8, then float32/255.0. Check shapes and dtypes at each step. Convert to PyTorch tensor. This is the exact preprocessing pipeline for any image model.
